narrative: Dear Alex, imagine the admissions team looking at your application like they're piecing together the story of your journey to becoming a lawyer, drawing from patterns they've seen in thousands of students who’ve walked this path before. Your Year 1 grades placed you in the 4th decile at school, which stood out as the strongest signal of concern—it suggested an early struggle to keep pace with the pack, much like students who've found the jump to law school rigor overwhelming. This was amplified by your Year 3 decile dropping to the 1st, painting a picture of grades slipping over time rather than building momentum, the second-biggest factor tipping the scales toward doubt about bar exam success. Your middle family income added another layer, as it's often linked to fewer resources for the intense prep that high-stakes exams demand. Even with a solid LSAT score of 35, which is respectable, it couldn't fully offset those trends, ranking as the fourth influence pulling toward a tougher road ahead. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.7 echoed that pattern of middling performance, while being a white male in a full-time program didn't shift the narrative in your favor here. Together, these elements created a profile that, in the model's view, carried a 52% chance of bar failure—close enough to the edge that they had to play it safe with spots so limited. It's not that you're not capable; it's that the story these details tell has matched too many others who hit roadblocks at the bar. The good news? You can rewrite this chapter—targeting those early grades or bolstering study habits could flip the script next time. Hang in there; law school's about persistence, and you've got the drive to prove them wrong.
